lines.text: APPENDIX
A note on names and dates.
The history of metabolic engineering is rich and the attribution of foundational contributions is sometimes contested.
I have tried to credit the most prominent figures fairly, but anyone with a deeper interest in the field's history should consult primary sources.
Jay Bailey and Gregory Stephanopoulos are widely credited with formally defining metabolic engineering as a named discipline in the early 1990s, through textbooks and review papers.
Jay Keasling and the artemisinin project at UC Berkeley are the most-cited demonstration of modern pathway engineering.
Christina Smolke's opioid yeast (2015) and Ron Milo's CO2-fixing E. coli (2019) are the canonical "wow" demonstrations of recent years.
Frances Arnold shared the 2018 Nobel in Chemistry for directed evolution, which is foundational across many enzyme engineering applications.
A note on companies.
I have mentioned a number of companies — Genentech, Amyris, Ginkgo Bioworks, Perfect Day, Geltor, Clara/EVERY, Motif FoodWorks, Genomatica, NatureWorks, Gevo, and others.
The financial and business histories of these companies are intricate, with mergers, restructurings, SPAC listings, and various pivots that are beyond the scope of a lecture script.
The mention of a company in this script reflects its scientific or technological importance to the field and is not an endorsement of its current commercial state or stock value.
If you are using any specific company information in a paper or report, please verify the current state with up-to-date sources.
A note on cross-references.
This lecture is part of a series.

The plasmids lecture covers the molecular biology of plasmids — origins of replication, selectable markers, restriction enzymes, cloning vectors, transformation — that sits underneath all of the genetic manipulations described here.

The genetic circuits lecture covers the engineering framework for thinking about biological systems as composable parts, devices, and circuits, which is the conceptual foundation for the modular cloning and AI-driven design segments.
The recombineering material covers MAGE and the multi-locus editing tools in more depth than was possible in Segment 9.
The CRISPR material covers the mechanism and tools of CRISPR in more depth than was possible in Segment 16.
Read these as a set for the fullest picture of how modern strain engineering operates.
A note on what was left out.
A two-hour lecture cannot cover everything.

Topics I touched only briefly or omitted include: mammalian cell metabolic engineering (CHO cells, hybridomas) for therapeutic antibody production; algae and cyanobacteria beyond a passing mention as photoautotrophic platforms; enzyme cascade engineering in vitro as an alternative to cellular fermentation; cell-free systems for protein production; mineral and metal biorecovery by engineered microbes; mycelium-based materials and the related fungal biotechnology; gut microbiome engineering for

therapeutic delivery; pharmaceutical-grade fermentation regulatory considerations (cGMP, ICH guidelines, FDA pathways for biological products); the economics of strain development costs versus product margins; intellectual property strategy in biotechnology; and the social and ethical implications of widely accessible strain engineering tools.
Each of these is a substantial topic worthy of its own lecture or course.

The student who wants to go deep into metabolic engineering should expect to spend years on the methodological details, the case studies, and the commercial and regulatory contexts that frame how the science gets translated into products.
A final note on the cell as factory.

The factory analogy I have used throughout — cell as chemical factory, enzymes as conveyor belts, feedstock as raw material, product as output — is a useful first approximation but, like all analogies, it eventually breaks.
Cells are not factories in any straightforward sense.
They evolved for fitness, not for product titer.
They are noisy in ways factories are not.
They reorganize themselves in response to environmental and internal cues, in ways factories cannot.

The whole engineering challenge of metabolic engineering is, in some sense, about negotiating with the cell — coaxing, pressuring, evolving, redesigning, until the cell's behavior aligns with the engineer's goals.
That negotiation is rarely complete and never permanent.

A strain engineered for high yield today will, if allowed to evolve under selective pressure, often slowly lose its engineered behavior over generations, because the engineered behavior is a fitness burden the wild-type configuration does not carry.
Maintaining engineered metabolism is an ongoing project, not a one-time accomplishment.
That is one of the deepest lessons the field has learned: cells push back.
The engineer's job is to push back well enough, with tools good enough, to keep the cell on the path the engineer chose.
The discipline is, at root, a particular kind of dialogue with life.
Doing it well requires both technical sophistication and a kind of humility about what you can and cannot make a cell do.
I will leave you with that thought.
Thank you.
